Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We need to parse the normative process description and event log. The normative process: steps in order: Receive order, Check inventory (must occur before payment authorization decisions), Authorize payment, Package items, Ship order, Close order. Also, if inventory insufficient, after Check inventory, go to Cancel order directly, then Close order. Thus the correct sequences are: Case with sufficient inventory: - Receive order - Check inventory - Authorize payment - Package items - Ship order - Close order Additionally any order where inventory insufficient: - Receive order - Check inventory - Cancel order - Close order Thus any deviation: e.g., Authorize payment before Check inventory, Packaging before Authorize, Shipping before Packaging, etc.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Case 2: - Receive order (OK) - Check inventory (OK) - Package items (This is after Check inventory but before Authorize payment—Violation because Authorize payment should come before Package items) - Authorize payment (After Package items - wrong order) - Ship order (After Authorize payment? Actually order: Package items, Authorize payment, Ship order - that would be correct if Authorize before Package, but we have Package before Authorize, so still violation) - Close order (after Ship order) - Close order should be final; its after Ship order, acceptable if Ship order done correctly but its not. However prior steps wrong. Thus Case 2 violates normative process.

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Case 3: - Receive order (OK) - Check inventory (OK) - Authorize payment (OK) (after Check inventory - good) - Cancel order (after Authorize payment? Actually after Authorize payment we go to Cancel order only if inventory insufficient; but we havent checked inventory sufficiency. At point after Authorize payment we dont know if inventory sufficient or not. The process: after Authorize payment, if inventory sufficient then Package items; if insufficient then Cancel order. Here we go to Cancel order directly after Authorize payment, implying inventory insufficient. But we never had a Check inventory step indicating sufficiency. However we did have Check inventory earlier (timestamp 09:20) but we dont know result. Usually Check inventory would determine sufficiency; we dont have that outcome recorded. The log jumps from Authorize payment to Cancel order, skipping Package items and Ship order entirely. This violates because if inventory sufficient we should have Package items then Ship order; if insufficient we should have Cancel order then Close order. Here we go Cancel order directly after Authorize payment, missing Package items and Ship order, and then Close order after Cancel? Actually after Cancel order we have Close order (timestamp 11:00) which is correct for insufficient case. However we missed Package items and Ship order entirely, which is a violation because those steps should occur if sufficient. Since we dont know sufficiency, but we jumped to Cancel order implying insufficient; still we omitted Package items and Ship order, which are required if sufficient. So violation. Thus Case 3 violates.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Case 4: - Receive order (OK) - Check inventory (OK) - Cancel order (after Check inventory? Actually timestamp 09:20 is Check inventory, next is Cancel order at 09:20? Wait case 4: timestamps: Receive 09:00, Check 09:10, Cancel 09:20, Close 09:50. So after Check inventory we go to Cancel order (implies inventory insufficient). Then Close order after Cancel order (good). No Package items, Ship order, Authorize payment steps missing entirely. This is the insufficient inventory path. Its correct per normative: Receive, Check, Cancel, Close. No violation.
